description: The figure displays engineering stress-strain curves of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250°C across different deformation passes. Four curves represent samples MAF-0 (solution-treated baseline, 0 pass), MAF-1 (1 pass, equivalent strain 0.47), MAF-3 (3 passes, equivalent strain 1.4), and MAF-5 (5 passes, equivalent strain 2.34). Caption and text extraction report that MAF-0 exhibits yield strength of 380 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 762 MPa, and total elongation of 75%. With increasing MAF passes, MAF-1 shows 1040 MPa yield strength and 30% elongation, MAF-3 reaches 1279 MPa yield strength with 13.7% elongation, and MAF-5 achieves 1528 MPa yield strength but only 8% elongation. The curves reveal a pronounced strength-ductility trade-off: yield strength quadruples while elongation decreases by nearly 90%, and work hardening capacity diminishes from 392 MPa to 20 MPa. This supports the core conclusion that progressive grain refinement (50 μm to 13 μm) and dislocation density accumulation (0.79×10¹⁵ m⁻² to 8.98×10¹⁵ m⁻²) dominate the strengthening mechanisms, validating the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model incorporating microstructural evolution.

This figure presents a schematic diagram illustrating the multiaxial forging (MAF) process sequence applied to Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel at 250°C. The diagram depicts the progressive deformation path starting from the original sample with initial dimensions of 20 × 20 × 30 mm, systematically showing three sequential compression stages (1st, 2nd, and 3rd compression) interspersed with 90° rotations about the y, z, and x axes. Each sub-illustration clearly indicates the dimensional changes and the reorientation of the orthogonal coordinate system (x, y, z) relative to the sample's colored faces (blue, green, orange). A central coordinate system defines three critical directions in the forging process: pressing direction (compression axis), constrained direction (restricted by die constraint), and flow direction (material expansion). The schematic demonstrates how the sample undergoes compression in three mutually perpendicular directions per pass with 33% normal strain (equivalent strain 0.47), accumulating severe plastic deformation through sequential rotation and recompression to achieve grain refinement.

Figure 2 presents photographic images of the forged billets and corresponding machined tensile specimens of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to multiaxial forging (MAF) for 1, 3, and 5 passes (MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5). The top row displays the macroscopic forged billets with handwritten markings (appearing as '1M', 'M1', and '5X' on the samples from left to right), while the bottom row shows the miniature round tensile samples with gauge sections machined from these billets. A metric ruler spanning 16–29 mm at the bottom provides dimensional reference, indicating billet lengths of approximately 15–20 mm. The samples exhibit metallic luster with varying surface discoloration that increases with deformation passes. The progression demonstrates the physical integrity of the steel after cumulative equivalent plastic strains of ~0.47, 1.4, and 2.34, respectively, confirming successful warm MAF processing at 250°C up to five passes before fracture occurred on the sixth attempt. This figure validates the sample preparation methodology for subsequent mechanical testing and microstructural characterization.

Figure 3 presents schematic diagrams illustrating the multiaxial forging (MAF) sample geometry and tensile specimen dimensions for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel. Panel (a) depicts a cubic MAF sample with overall dimensions of 30 mm × 20 mm × 20 mm, highlighting the central analyzed area designated for XRD and EBSD characterization, alongside orthogonal direction annotations including flow direction, pressing direction, and constrained direction. Panel (b) specifies the miniature round tensile specimen geometry featuring a 16 mm gauge length, 26.5 mm total length, 4 mm gauge diameter, and R1 fillet radius. These diagrams standardize the sampling methodology—tensile specimens extracted from consistent core locations at MAF-0, MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5 stages—and establish the coordinate system essential for texture analysis. The figure supports the experimental design for correlating microstructural evolution (grain size, dislocation density) with mechanical properties across equivalent strains of 0, 0.47, 1.4, and 2.34 during warm MAF processing at 250°C.

The figure presents a half-sectional view of a 3-D finite element model simulating the multiaxial forging (MAF) process of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel at 250°C using ABAQUS/Explicit. The left subfigure shows the initial configuration before compression, featuring a rectangular deformable workpiece (blue mesh with fine grid) enclosed by rigid die walls (gray mesh). The right subfigure illustrates the deformed geometry after compression, demonstrating significant barrel-shaped expansion in the X-direction (flow direction) and compression in the Y-direction (height direction) while maintaining constraint in the Z-direction. The simulation employs C3D8R hexahedral elements with an optimal mesh size of 0.6 mm (41,382 elements total), reduced integration, and hourglass control. Boundary conditions include a controlled 10 mm displacement of the punch in the Y-direction, fixed die walls, and a friction coefficient of 0.15 at the workpiece-die interface. This visualization validates the finite element setup for investigating plastic flow behavior using a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model that incorporates evolution of dislocation density and grain size, supporting the simulation of steady-state MAF processes.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel at different multiaxial forging (MAF) passes, specifically comparing samples MAF-0 (solutionized initial state), MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5. The patterns are plotted as intensity versus 2θ angle from 40° to 120°. MAF-0 exhibits sharp diffraction peaks indexed as austenite γ-phase (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), along with κ-carbide peaks (220). As MAF passes increase, significant peak broadening and intensity reduction occur progressively from MAF-0 to MAF-5, indicating grain refinement and increased dislocation density. The caption and citation context confirm these patterns were used to calculate dislocation density and microstrain, showing values increasing from 0.79×10¹⁵ m⁻² (MAF-0) to 8.98×10¹⁵ m⁻² (MAF-5). The figure also verifies that carbide phases remain unchanged during deformation, supporting the conclusion that precipitation strengthening remains constant while grain boundary and dislocation strengthening dominate the mechanical property evolution during warm multiaxial forging at 250°C.

Figure 6 illustrates the evolution of dislocation density as a function of equivalent plastic strain for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250°C. The graph plots experimentally measured dislocation density values (black squares) obtained from XRD analysis of samples after 0, 1, 3, and 5 MAF passes—corresponding to equivalent plastic strains of 0, 0.47, 1.4, and 2.34, respectively—alongside a theoretical prediction curve (red dashed line) calculated using Eq. (7): ρ = ρ₀ + k₁(1-exp(-k₂ε)). The inset table provides the nonlinear curve fitting parameters: ρ₀ = 7.96×10¹⁴ m⁻², k₁ = (7.90516±0.528171)×10¹⁵ m⁻², and k₂ = 1.81319±0.42469, with a coefficient of determination (R-Square) of 0.98554. The data reveals that dislocation density increases rapidly during initial deformation and asymptotically approaches saturation, rising from approximately 0.79×10¹⁵ m⁻² in the solutionized initial state to about 8.98×10¹⁵ m⁻² after five passes. This quantitative relationship between dislocation density and plastic strain is fundamental to the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model developed in the study, enabling accurate prediction of flow stress and yield strength through finite element simulations by incorporating strain-dependent dislocation strengthening mechanisms.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) analysis of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250°C. Subfigures (a)-(d) display image quality maps overlaid with grain boundaries for samples MAF-0, MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5, where green lines denote low-angle boundaries (1°-15° misorientation) and red lines indicate high-angle boundaries (15°-180°). The fraction of high-angle boundaries decreases from 0.62 in the solutionized MAF-0 sample to 0.29 (MAF-1) and 0.24 (MAF-3), then increases significantly to 0.69 after 5 passes as some low-angle boundaries convert to high-angle boundaries. Subfigures (e)-(h) show inverse pole figure (IPF) maps of the normal (pressing) direction, revealing that the initial strong [101] texture in MAF-0 becomes increasingly randomized with additional MAF passes. These observations demonstrate the evolution of dislocation substructures, grain refinement from 50 μm to approximately 13 μm, and progressive texture randomization during severe plastic deformation.

Figure 8 presents quantitative microstructural evolution data for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel during warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250°C. Subfigure (a) displays area fraction versus grain size distributions obtained from EBSD analysis, comparing four processing conditions: the solutionized/quenched initial state (MAF-0, ~50 μm) and samples subjected to 1, 3, and 5 MAF passes (MAF-1, MAF-3, MAF-5). The distributions demonstrate progressive grain refinement toward smaller sizes (~13 μm after 5 passes) and narrowing of the size distribution with increasing deformation. Subfigure (b) plots measured average grain size against equivalent plastic strain, showing an exponential decay relationship fitted by d = 50×exp(−0.64ε) with R² = 0.991. The data validates the grain size evolution model (Eq. 8) incorporated into the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model, confirming that grain refinement follows an exponential function of imposed strain during the MAF process. These results support the integration of strain-dependent microstructural parameters (grain size and dislocation density) for predicting flow behavior in the low-density steel.

This figure presents Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps obtained via Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) showing the evolution of geometrically necessary dislocation density in Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250°C. The four subfigures display the KAM distribution for (a) MAF-0 (solutionized, 0 passes, KAM_avg = 0.34°), (b) MAF-1 (1 pass, KAM_avg = 0.72°), (c) MAF-3 (3 passes, KAM_avg = 0.87°), and (d) MAF-5 (5 passes, KAM_avg = 1.07°). All maps share a 60 μm scale bar and employ a unique color scale ranging from 0° (blue, low misorientation) to 5° (red, high misorientation). A progressive transition from predominantly blue (low dislocation density) to mixed green-yellow-red patterns (high dislocation density) is observed with increasing MAF passes. This visual evidence confirms the accumulation of dislocations as cumulative equivalent strain increases from 0 to 2.34, supporting the dislocation strengthening mechanism and validating the modified Johnson-Cook model's prediction of microstructural evolution during severe plastic deformation.

The figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF) for 0, 1, 3, and 5 passes, labeled as MAF-0 (grey), MAF-1 (red), MAF-3 (blue), and MAF-5 (green), respectively. The solution-treated coarse-grained sample (MAF-0) exhibits the lowest yield strength of approximately 380 MPa and ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 762 MPa with extensive ductility exceeding 70% strain. As MAF progresses, significant strengthening occurs: MAF-1 shows yield strength of 1040 MPa and UTS of 1368 MPa, while MAF-3 reaches 1279 MPa and 1415 MPa respectively. After 5 passes (MAF-5), yield strength dramatically increases to 1528 MPa with UTS of 1548 MPa, indicating minimal work hardening capacity (only 20 MPa). Concurrently, uniform elongation decreases substantially from 63% (MAF-0) to 0.8% (MAF-5). The curves demonstrate that increasing MAF passes progressively enhance strength through dislocation density accumulation and grain refinement but severely diminish plasticity and strain hardening capability, consistent with the microstructural evolution described in the text.

This figure presents the execution flow chart of the VUMAT (Vectorized User MATerial) subroutine developed for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel to implement a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model in ABAQUS/Explicit finite element software. The flow begins with subroutine initialization and material constant computation, followed by a time-stepping loop. At each increment, the algorithm checks if step time equals zero: if yes, it calculates trial stress assuming pure elastic material; if no, it computes the deviatoric trial stress difference (sigdif). When sigdif exceeds zero, stresses are updated via the constitutive relation; otherwise, equivalent plastic strain is set to zero. The subroutine subsequently updates six state-dependent variables (SDVs) tracking equivalent plastic strain, dislocation density, grain size, and flow stress, followed by updating internal and dissipated inelastic energy. This flow chart illustrates the computational framework for incorporating microstructural evolution (dislocation density and grain refinement) into finite element simulations of warm multiaxial forging (MAF) processes.

This figure presents finite element simulation results showing the evolution of microstructural and mechanical field variables in Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel during warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250°C across Pass 1, Pass 3, and Pass 5. The four subfigures display: (a) equivalent strain distribution maps showing progressive strain accumulation from the surface toward the center with increasing passes; (b) dislocation density distribution maps (scale in m⁻²) revealing enhanced dislocation generation and density buildup in central regions as deformation proceeds; (c) grain size distribution maps (scale in μm) demonstrating grain refinement with inverse correlation to dislocation density, where finer grains appear in high-strain central areas and coarser grains remain at outer regions; and (d) flow stress distribution maps (scale in MPa) indicating marked stress level increases correlated with strain hardening and dislocation accumulation. The simulation, implemented via a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model incorporating Eqs. (7) and (8) in ABAQUS/Explicit VUMAT subroutine, captures the spatial heterogeneity of deformation and validates the relationship between equivalent strain, dislocation density evolution, grain refinement, and flow stress development during severe plastic deformation.

This line graph compares experimental and finite element (FE) simulated yield strength values for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250°C over multiple deformation passes. The x-axis shows the number of MAF passes (0, 1, 3, 5), while the y-axis displays yield strength in MPa (ranging from 200 to 1600 MPa). Solid squares represent experimental measurements, and dashed circles denote FE simulation results using a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model that incorporates strain-dependent dislocation density and grain size evolution. Both curves demonstrate a monotonic increase in yield strength with pass number: from approximately 380 MPa at pass 0, rising sharply to around 1040 MPa (experimental) and 940 MPa (simulation) at pass 1, then increasing gradually to roughly 1279 MPa at pass 3 for both, and reaching about 1528 MPa (experimental) versus 1450 MPa (simulation) at pass 5. The close agreement up to three passes validates the model's incorporation of dislocation strengthening and grain refinement mechanisms, while the slight underprediction at five passes—attributed to the model's omission of texture effects—highlights the increasing contribution of grain boundary strengthening at higher strain levels.

Figure 14 presents finite element simulation results showing yield strength distribution maps (in MPa) for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF). The image displays three pairs of contour plots viewed from different orientations (X-Y-Z coordinate systems indicated), representing progressive deformation stages corresponding to MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5 passes (left to right). The color scale (SDV5) ranges from 699.4 MPa (dark blue) to 1358 MPa (red), illustrating the predicted yield strength values based on a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model integrating strain-dependent dislocation density and grain size evolution. The maps reveal increasing yield strength with MAF passes, with maximum values concentrated at the sample center (red zones in the rightmost pair) and decreasing toward surfaces (blue/green regions), confirming the depth-dependent strengthening behavior where cumulative strain effects are most pronounced in the interior.
